segments.s189-g73: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so,” placed before the clause to mark manner. داستان زد is a poetic idiom: زد is the past-tense of زدن used with داستان to mean “told a tale” (here the verb precedes its subject by poetic inversion). یکی پرخرد is an indefinite subject phrase: یکی “a certain/one” + پرخرد “very wise” (an adjective compound meaning “full of wisdom”), placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s189-g74: که introduces the reported-content clause (“that…”). از خوی بد is a prepositional phrase ‘from/because of a bad disposition’ (خوی = disposition/habit, بد = bad), with the adjective following the noun as is common in Persian poetry. کوه کیفر برد is the clause’s core: کوه (the mountain) is the subject, کیفر (punishment) is the object, and برد is the past of بردن used here as ‘took/received’ — so ‘the mountain received punishment.’ The word order and verb form are idiomatic/poetic.
segments.s189-g65: چو ... شد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when'. از دور نزدیک شد is literally 'became near from afar' — classical Persian uses شدن + adjective to mean 'to become/come [adjective]', so نزدیک شد = 'approached/was come near'. ریونیز is the subject/name placed after the predicate (verb-final name), a typical poetic word order.
segments.s189-g66: بـرکشید is a past simple verb 'lifted/drew up' and here governs the direct object phrase that follows. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', خمانیده is an adjective 'bent/curved' modifying شیز (likely a personal name or epithet). بزه is uncertain in meaning in this context (see senses); here برکشید most plausibly means 'drew (a weapon)'.
segments.s189-g67: ز بالا is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from above'. خدنگی is the instrument (a dagger) and بزد is the past tense verb 'he struck/smote'. بر is the preposition 'upon/at' and برش here functions as the target/object of the blow (the exact referent of برش is ambiguous in the manuscript).
segments.s189-g68: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/which', and دوخت is the past of دوختن 'to sew/ stitch' used literally here. با + ترگ رومی indicates the instrument/means 'with a Roman rag/shred', and سر + ش (the enclitic pronoun ش = 'his') is the affected noun phrase 'his head'. Grammatically this clause describes the consequent condition of the blow.
segments.s189-g69: بیفتاد is a past tense verb 'he fell'. و connects clauses. برگشت here means 'turned back' or 'recoiled'. ز introduces the agent/source 'from him' and اسپ تیز is a noun phrase 'a swift horse' (اسپ = steed, تیز = 'fast/keen'), with the adjective following the noun as is common.
segments.s189-g70: بخاک اندر آمد is a fixed poetic expression 'came down to the earth' (i.e., fell to the ground): بخاک = 'to the ground', اندر = adverbial/inchoative particle 'in/into', آمد = 'came'. سر ریو نیز is the noun phrase 'the head of Rīvū also' (سر = head, ریو here a name/identifier or 'face', and نیز = 'also').
segments.s189-g71: ببالا 'upwards' + چو 'when' starts a temporal clause. طوس is the proper name 'Tūs'. از می‌م is an archaic locative/pointing expression (می‌م here is an older form cognate to 'there/where he is'), so از می‌م بنگرید = 'he looked from where he stood'. بنگرید is the past-subjunctive/past-simple literary form of نگریستن 'to look/gaze'.
segments.s189-g72: شد is the past of شدن 'became' used here as an intransitive resultative auxiliary: شد آن کوه بر چشم او ناپدید = 'that mountain became unseen to his sight'. بر چشم او is a dative-like expression meaning 'to his eyes / in his sight', and ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished/unseen'.
segments.s189-g57: This is a conditional / circumstantial clause with a vocative at the end. بتیر (preposed) is a means phrase 'with/by an arrow'; اسپ بیجان is the object phrase 'the steed lifeless' (adjective بیجان after the noun); کنم‌گر is an archaic sequence: کنَم + گر = 'if I do' (گر is the conditional particle attached to the verb), and سوار at the very end is a vocative 'O rider'. Word order: [instrument] [noun + predicate] [verb+if‑particle] [vocative].
segments.s189-g58: چه گویی is an interrogative phrase 'what do you say' (چه = 'what', گویی = 'you say' / 'would you say'); تو‌ای is the vocative 'you, O …' (تو + ای). کار دیده is a nominal phrase literally 'deed-seen' i.e. 'one who has seen deeds' or 'of experienced deed' (دیده here works as a participial/adjectival element); تخوار is a pejorative noun 'wretch/lowly one' addressing the addressee. Overall this segment is an address-question: 'what do you say, you wretch who has seen (deeds)?'
segments.s189-g59: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' as indirect object pronoun + گفت = 'said'); بر مرد بگشای بر is an imperative/command phrase 'open/strike upon the man' where بر = 'against/on', مرد = 'the man', and بگشای (imperative/infinitive form of گشودن) = 'open/attack'. The repeated بر after the verb is a poetic reinforcement of the prepositional sense 'on/against'.
segments.s189-g60: مگر here introduces a cautionary purpose 'lest' / 'for fear that' (not the interrogative 'isn't it?'). طوس را is the definite object/topic 'Tus' with the object marker را. ز + و (written here as separate tokens ز and و = ز او) = 'from him' / 'because of him' (poetic contracted form). بسوزد is a present/jussive subjunctive 'may burn' or 'may cause to ache' (expressing the feared result), and جگر is used figuratively 'heart/seat of feeling' (i.e. 'his heart').
segments.s189-g61: بداند is a present/factual verb 'he knows' (3rd person); که introduces the content clause 'that'. تو is the nominative 'you'. دل بیاراستی is a verbless predicate phrase 'you arranged/straightened the heart' — idiomatically 'you were sincere/your heart was set right'. بیاراستی is an older/poetic form and functions adjectivally/verbal here (the provided sense list doesn't align exactly with this usage).
segments.s189-g62: که again = 'that' (introducing the subordinate clause). بااو = با + او 'with him'. همی is the old verbal particle that gives an ongoing or emphatic sense to the verb (here marking the attempted action). آشتی = 'reconciliation' (noun) and خواستی is past-tense 'you desired/wished'. Together: 'that you wished to make peace with him.'
segments.s189-g63: چنین = 'thus/so'. با تو = 'with you' or 'against you' (با + تو; in context it means 'directed at you'). بر (here) = 'against' and خیره is an adjective 'astonished/startled' modifying the manner of the attack (a sudden/astonishing assault). جنگ آورد is a verbal phrase 'brought about war' (آورد = 'caused/brought about'); word order is subject‑omitted + manner phrase + verb 'آورد'.
segments.s189-g64: همی (here) is a connective/continuative particle ('and thus' / 'thereby'); بر برادرت = 'upon your brother' (بر + برادر + ت, where ت is the 2nd-person possessive suffix on برادر). ننگ = 'shame/disgrace' and آورد = 'brought' (cause/result). The whole phrase: 'and thereby he brought shame on your brother.'
segments.s189-g33: بیامد is a past-tense, third-person singular verb meaning “came.” دگرباره is an adverb placed after the verb to mark repetition (“again”). داماد طوس is a noun phrase (داماد = “groom/son‑in‑law”) followed by its possessor طوس (Tūs); classical Persian permits this noun + possessor order without an explicit ezāfe in epic diction.
segments.s189-g34: همی کرد uses همی as a progressive/continuous marker (“kept on”) with the past کرد (“did/made”), so the clause means a repeated or ongoing action. گردون is the affected domain (the heavens/fate) and the phrase برو بر فسوس is a compact expression where برو (go) + بر (on/against/toward) link to فسوس (whisper/idle talk) as the manner or cause; overall the verb predicates that he continually caused the heavens to be disturbed by whispers (فسوس).
segments.s189-g35: ز marks origin/adverbial source (“from”) and introduces the route phrase ز راه چرم (“from the road of Charm”). بر + سپدکوه indicates direction/toward Sepdkuh. شد is the past of شدن used to mark motion or arrival (“went/arrived/was made”) at the end of the clause, as is common in epic narrative.
segments.s189-g36: دلش is the noun دل with the enclitic possessive ش = “his.” پرجفا is a descriptive adjective/compound placed before the copula, and بود is the past copula “was” (stative). نستوه شد is a result clause: نستوه (“distressed, overwhelmed”) followed by شد (“became”), so the sequence states a mental condition and then the change into it.
segments.s189-g37: چو is the subordinating temporal particle “when.” از introduces the source phrase از تیغ بالا (“from the high sword/blade”), and فرودش is a verbal noun or noun of action (فرود = “descent/fall”) with the enclitic ش = “him/it,” so the clause reads roughly “when he saw his descent from the high blade.” بدید is the past tense “he saw” closing the temporal clause.
segments.s189-g38: ز marks source/starting point; قربان here is a qualifier/phrase attached before the noun phrase and can be read as a noun or descriptive element (“sacrifice”/“sacrificial” in form), while کمان کیان is a genitive construction meaning “the bow of the Kians/the royal bow.” برکشید is a past transitive verb meaning “drew up” or “drew (the bow).” The verb at the end follows the customary subject‑object‑verb order of classical Persian.
segments.s189-g39: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so.” گفت is the past verb “said/spoke” (here used as the reporting verb). با رزم دیده is a prepositional description: با = “with,” رزم دیده literally “battle‑seen” (a participial compound meaning ‘battle‑hardened’ or ‘veteran’), and تخوار functions as the name/epithet of the speaker placed after the descriptive phrase.
segments.s189-g40: که introduces a subordinate clause (“for/that”). طوس is the subject (Tūs). آن سخنها is the object phrase “those words” (آن = that/those + سخنها = speeches/words). گرفتست is a perfective/passive‑like form meaning “was taken/was affected” and is followed by خوار as the predicate adjective meaning “humbled/despised,” so the clause says Tūs was made low/ashamed by those words.
segments.s189-g41: که introduces the reported clause ('that'). آمد سواری is an inverted, poetic order (verb before noun) meaning 'a rider came.' و connects the coordinate clause that follows. بهرام نیست uses the present copula نیست to state 'Bahram is not (here/present).'
segments.s189-g42: مرا دل درشتست is an older/dialectal word order where مرا functions as a dative/possessive 'for me' (roughly 'I have'), دل is the grammatical subject and درشتست = درشت + ست (adjective + copula) 'is large/stout.' و پدرام نیست is a coordinated clause: 'and Pedram is not (present).' The copula -ست is attached to the adjective (poetic fusion).
segments.s189-g43: ببین is an imperative 'look/see.' تا introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that'). مگر here is a modal/rhetorical particle (used like 'perhaps'/'perchance'). یادت آید is a light-subjunctive/optative expression literally 'your memory comes' = 'you remember.' که کیست is an embedded interrogative ('who he is').
segments.s189-g44: سراپای در آهن is a noun phrase meaning 'head-to-toe in iron' (سراپا + prepositional phrase در آهن). از بهر چیست is a rhetorical question 'for what purpose/what is it for?' where چی + ست (چیست) asks 'what is it' and از بهر adds 'for the sake of/for what reason.' Word order places the copula/stative particle after the interrogative (poetic).
segments.s189-g45: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' داد is past 'gave' and پاسـخ is its object 'an answer.' مر is an older dative clitic 'to me' (here marking the beneficiary/indirect object), او را is the definite/direct object 'him,' and تخوار (wretch) is the subject placed after the verb by poetic inversion — literally 'thus gave answer to me him the wretch.'
segments.s189-g46: که introduces the content of the reply 'that...'. این ریونیزست contains an archaic/adjectival form plus the copula -ست fused to the word (i.e. 'this is ریونیز-'). گرد و سوار are coordinated predicates: گرد used adjectivally ('stout/round/robust' in context) and سوار 'mounted/horseman.' The clause therefore states an identification and two coordinated attributes.
segments.s189-g47: چهل خواهرستش should be read as خواهر استش (forty sisters he has) with the copula/possessive fused in the ending -ستش — an older poetic compression meaning 'he has forty sisters.' چو introduces a simile 'like/as,' so چو خرم بهار = 'like the joyful spring.'
segments.s189-g48: پسر خود is 'his own son' (خود emphasizing possession). جزین نیست is an existential negative 'there is none except this' (جز + این preceding the copula نیست). اندر تبار is a locative/genitive phrase 'in the lineage/line,' so the whole clause says there is no other son in his line except this one.
segments.s189-g9: بدو گفت بهرام is a classical-word-order clause: بدو (‘to him’) is an enclitic/prepositional pronoun placed before the verb گفت (‘said’), so literally “to him said Bahrām.” کای is the vocative particle “O,” and پهلوان is the vocative noun “champion/hero.”
segments.s189-g10: مکن is the negative imperative “do not.” هیچ functions as an intensifier here (“at all/never”). تیره روان is a compound meaning roughly “dark/ill‑fated soul” (تیره = bleak/ominous, روان = soul/spirit). برخیره is an archaic/poetic particle here linking the negated action to the result (literally something like “bring/put upon”); the whole string means “do nothing that will bring a dark fate on your soul.”
segments.s189-g11: بترس is the imperative “fear” or “be afraid (of).” از introduces the object of fear (“of/from”). خداوند here reads as God or sovereign; خورشید و ماه are poetic epithets ‘sun and moon’ (parallel objects joined by و). Word order is standard: imperative + از + objects.
segments.s189-g12: دلت را is دل + second‑person suffix ت = “your heart,” with را marking it as the direct object. بشرم آور here is a verbal phrase: بشرم (a noun/abstract meaning ‘pity/compassion’ in this context) + آور (“bring”) — literally “bring pity to your heart.” از روی شاه = “because of/at the sight of the king” (از + روی = “from/at the face/sight of”).
segments.s189-g13: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “for/that/whose.” پیوند اویست should be read as “he is his bond” (پیوند = bond, و/است contracted as اویست in older diction) and همزاد اوی = “and his twin” (همزاد = twin; اوی = his/that one). This is archaic syntax where the copula and possessive pronouns are compacted.
segments.s189-g14: This is a predicative description: سواریست is a nominal predicate ‘is a horseman/rider’ (compound with the older -است), نام‌آور is an attributive adjective ‘renowned/famed,’ and جنگ‌جوی is another coordinate predicate ‘and a warrior.’ Word order places the nominal predicate before its modifiers in characteristic classical style.
segments.s189-g15: که‌گر is a conditional conjunction meaning “if.” یک سوار از میان سپاه = “a single/one rider from among the army” (سوار = rider; از میان = from the middle/among; سپاه = army). Word order places the quantified noun (یک سوار) before the postpositional phrase of source.
segments.s189-g16: شود is the present/future auxiliary “becomes/goes” or “will go” here (“goes/comes”). نزد means “to/in the presence of” (i.e., toward), آن = “that,” پرهنر = “skillful/such a one of great deeds,” پور = “son/descendant” (a name‑forming/relational element), and شاه = “king.” Together: “goes to that skilled son of the king.”
segments.s189-g17: ز here is the archaic/prepositional 'from'. چنگش = چنگ + ـش is a possessed noun 'his grip/claw'. رهایی is the noun 'release/escape'. نیابد is a classical negative third-person singular ('does not/ cannot obtain' or 'is not found'); together رهایی نیابد = 'finds no escape' or 'does not escape'. بجان is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'with life' / 'alive' (literally 'by the life'), so the whole clause reads 'from his grip one does not escape alive'.
segments.s189-g18: غم is the noun 'sorrow'. آری here is the poetic/older-form verbal/adverbial element (in classical verse often serving like آید/'comes' or as an intensifier 'indeed'); همی is the archaic present/progressive particle (like modern می) marking ongoing or inevitable action. بر دل = 'upon the heart', شادمان = adjective 'joyful' or 'glad'. The clause therefore means 'sorrow will (indeed) come upon the joyful heart.'
segments.s189-g19: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (military commander). شد + predicate آشفته = 'became disturbed/upset'. از before گفت expresses cause/agent 'because of / by (his) speech'. گفت is used nominally here 'what was said / his words'. اوی is the pronominal clitic referring to 'him' (here functioning possessively: 'his words'). So: 'The spahbed became disturbed by his words.'
segments.s189-g20: نبد is an archaic negative/existential 'was not / there was not'. پند is the noun 'advice' or 'counsel' and is its logical direct object ('there was no counsel'). The following phrase 'بهرام یل جفت اوی' is an apposition: بهرام (name) + یل (epithet 'hero') and جفت اوی = 'a pair/match for him' (جفت used as 'match/pair'). Put together the line says effectively 'There was no counsel — for Bahram the hero there was no match (i.e. no suitable counsel).' Grammatically, نبد introduces an absence and the following noun phrase specifies what is lacking.
segments.s189-g21: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here introduces a purpose/subjunctive clause ('that/so that'). نامبردار is 'the aforementioned/named ones' (the persons just referred to). چند quantifies them 'a few/several'. Together بفرمود تا نامبردار چند = 'he ordered that several of the named (men)...'.
segments.s189-g22: بتازند is the present/subjunctive 3rd-pl form of تاختن with the prefixed ب- (بـ + تازند), meaning 'let them ride/let them rush/they should attack'; the -ند ending marks 3rd plural. نزدیک is the preposition/adverb 'near/toward'. کوه بلند is the noun phrase 'the high/tall mountain'. The full clause (subjunctive introduced by تا) reads 'that several of the named should ride up near the high mountain.'
segments.s189-g23: ز = 'from' (poetic preposition). گردان فراوان is a prepositional noun phrase 'the numerous troop(s)/a large force' (گردان = troop, فراوان = plentiful/ many). برون تاختند = 'they rode out / rushed forth' (برون = out/forth, تاختند = past plural 'they rushed/attacked'). Word order places the source phrase before the verb: 'from a great host they rushed forth.'
segments.s189-g24: نبرد is the noun 'battle/war'. وراگردن here is a compound/old verbal phrase meaning 'to turn toward / direct against' (vora- + گردن). افراختند is the past plural 'they raised/erected' (from افراز/افراشتن). Grammatically نبرد is the object/activity and وراگردن افراختند means 'they raised/turned (the) battle toward (him)', i.e. 'they set about battle and directed it against him.'
segments.s189-g25: This is a reporting clause in classical Persian word order: indirect object + manner + verb + speaker. بدیشان (to them) is the indirect object, چنین (thus) marks the manner of speaking, گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used as speech-reporting), and بهرام گرد is the name of the speaker (Bahrām with the epithet/element گرد). The verb precedes the proper name because Persian allows flexible order in poetry.
segments.s189-g26: This is the content clause introduced by که (that). این کار (this affair/deed) is the topic; یکسر (entirely/utterly) is an adverb intensifier modifying مدارید. مدارید is an exhortative/imperative or jussive idea meaning 'show leniency / spare' (here the sense is 'be lenient'), and خرد appears as a vocative or address ('O wise ones' / 'men of wisdom') — a compressed, poetic way of addressing the listeners.
segments.s189-g27: بدان is an imperative/lexicalized 'know that' built from ب‑ + دان (here split in the gloss). کوه سر خویش is a compressed genitival phrase meaning 'that mountain is his head' (خوی + ش = خویش = 'his own'); کیخسروست is the copular predicate 'is Kay Khosrow'. The overall order is typical of poetic Persian: imperative verb + topic + predicate.
segments.s189-g28: که introduces an explanatory clause ('that...'). یک موی او = 'one hair of him' (موی = hair, او = his). به ز is a classical comparative formula ('better than / worth more than'), where به + ز together form the comparison. صد پهلوست = 'a hundred flanks' (پهلوست = flank/side); the clause states a hyperbolic comparison praising Kay Khosrow's worth.
segments.s189-g29: هران کس که ... بدید is a standard relative/indefinite construction: 'whoever/person that ... saw'. هران here distributes 'each/ whoever', کس is 'person', که introduces the relative clause, روی سیاوش is the direct object 'Siavash's face', and بدید is the simple past 'saw'.
segments.s189-g30: This clause states the effect of that sight. نیارد is an archaic/poetic form whose glosses given do not match the verbal sense here (so its specific sense is marked unknown); ز دیدار او = 'from the sight of him' (ز = from, دیدار = seeing/sight, او = him). آرمید is past 'rested' or 'found rest'. The clause thus says that after seeing him the viewer did not attain rest (the negative/nuanced meaning of نیارد must be read in the poetic context).
segments.s189-g31: چو = 'when'. This temporal clause: when Bahram (بهرام) داد (gave) این نشان (this sign) از فرود ('of descent' / 'that he would descend') — فرود and نشان together mean 'the signal to descend' or 'a sign of coming down'. داد is simple past 'gave'.
segments.s189-g32: ز ره بازگشتند = 'returned from the road' (ز = from, ره = way/road, بازگشتند = they returned/turned back, past plural). گردنکشان is an epithet meaning 'the proud-necked' or 'rebels' (those who lift their necks), functioning as the subject/agent in apposition — poetic epithets often follow the verb in this type of line.
segments.s189-g49: A coordinate list of epithets joined by و. The three items (فریبنده, ریمن, چاپلوس) form a single nominal phrase describing the person; و simply links the items. In this line ریمن appears as a name or label inserted between two adjectival/descriptive words.
segments.s189-g50: This is a small predicative clause: دلیر و جوانست = 'is brave and young' (جوانست = جوان است, the verb است suffixed). The second و links another nominal phrase: داماد طوس = 'son‑in‑law of Tūs' (the ezāfe between داماد and طوس is omitted in the poetry).
segments.s189-g51: چنین گفت ... فرود = 'Farud said thus to the discerning man.' Word order is poetic: گفت is the verb 'said' (here used with چنین 'thus'); با + noun marks the person addressed or accompaniment ('to/with the man'). بینا 'discerning' modifies مرد. Note: فرود here is the name (placed at the end for poetic effect).
segments.s189-g52: که introduces a reported/subordinate clause 'that ...'. هنگام جنگ = 'at the time of battle' (هنگام + noun). این نباید شنود = 'this must not be heard' — نباید + present/subjunctive شنود expresses prohibition/impersonal obligation (archaic/poetic 3rd‑person form شنود = 'hear').
segments.s189-g53: چو آید = 'when he comes' (چو = 'when', آید = 3rd‑sg. 'comes'); به پیکار = 'to/into battle' (به + noun indicating direction/scene). کنداوران is the following noun phrase linked to پیکار (either a group-name or genitive); its precise sense is obscure here and behaves as the complement of 'battle'.
segments.s189-g54: بخوابمش = b + خوابانیدن + object enclitic = 'I will put him to sleep' (in poetry a euphemism that can mean 'kill him'); the final -ش (here written -مش) is the clitic 'him'. بر دامن خواهران is a locative phrase 'upon the laps/skirts of the sisters' (بر = on/upon, دامن = lap/hem, خواهران = sisters).
segments.s189-g55: This clause is compact and somewhat idiomatic/poetic: بدو‌گر = 'an ill‑doer / malefactor'; کند باد is an optative/inversion where باد expresses a wishing/optative sense ('may he do'); کلکم = 'my stratagem/plan' (or 'my pen' in other senses); گذار here functions like 'let (it) pass / put aside' — the precise verbal sense is poetic. Overall: 'If a malefactor should do (it), may my stratagem be put aside.'
segments.s189-g56: اگر زنده ماند = 'if he remains alive' (اگر 'if', زنده 'alive', ماند 'remains'). بمردم مدار is an elliptical admonition: بمردم here uses the verb 'to die / perish' (inflected/formed as a verbal phrase), and مدار in the manuscript sense list does not match the idiomatic meaning in the line — contextually it means 'do not reckon/treat me' (so here overall: 'do not count me among the living/dead' depending on context).
segments.s189-g1: وزان پس چنین گفت با سرکشان is a short clause describing a speech act. وزان پس (literally “from that time/thereupon, then”) is a temporal/conclusive phrase placed before the verb. چنین گفت = “said thus” (چنین “thus” modifying گفت); گفت is the verbal root meaning ‘said’ (past). با + NP (با سرکشان) marks the audience (’to/with the rebellious/proud ones’) — in classical usage با can mark the addressees.
segments.s189-g2: This is the vocative/exclamatory address. که‌ای here functions as an archaic vocative/particle introducing the address (classical that/’O’); نامداران and گردنکشان are stacked nouns describing the addressees: نامداران “the renowned/illustrious ones” and گردنکشان “the proud/neck‑lifting ones” (plural). Word order is vocative particle + epithets.
segments.s189-g3: یکی نامور خواهم و نامجوی is a coordinated predicate describing what the speaker will do. یکی (indefinite “one / someone”) is the (implicit) object, followed by two predicative adjectives/nominal phrases: نامور “renowned” and نامجوی “seeking a name / fame-seeking.” خواهم (first-person) is the modal/auxiliary meaning “I will / I intend,” so the whole means “I will make someone renowned and name-seeking.”
segments.s189-g4: This is a relative/restrictive clause introduced by کز (classical form meaning “who / from whom / that”), which links back to ‘one’ in the previous segment. ایدر = “here”; نهد is a 3rd-person singular verb “sets/places / turns” (classical present/future sense: ‘will set/turn’); سوی + NP marks direction (“toward”); آن ترک روی = “that Turk (literally: ‘that Turk’s face’/‘that Turk’)” — together: “who from here will turn his face toward that Turk.”
segments.s189-g5: This clause uses the object-marker را after سرش (“his head”) to mark the definite direct object. سرش = head + possessive enclitic. ببرد is a 3rd-person form meaning “takes away / will take off” (here ‘cut off’ or ‘remove’); بخنجر is an instrumental prepositional/adverbial phrase ‘with a dagger’; ز تن = ‘from the body’ indicating separation. Word order: object (سرش را) + verb (ببرد) + instrument and origin.
segments.s189-g6: بپیش من is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘before me / in my presence’ (بپیش = before/in front of). آرد is the 3rd-person singular verb “brings.” بدین انجمن = ‘to this assembly’ (بدین = to/into this; انجمن = assembly/meeting). The sequence therefore expresses the intended action: ‘bring it before me into this assembly.’
segments.s189-g7: میان را ببست اندران ریونیز: میان را (’the middle’ marked by را as definite object) is the thing acted on; ببست is the past tense ‘he closed/shut/bound’; اندر(ان) = ‘among/therein’ situates the action among them or in that place; ریونیز is an obscure or proper name (here probably a proper noun or rare term) functioning as the instrument or agentive/associative element. The clause reads ‘he closed the middle among them there with/through Riyuniz.’
segments.s189-g8: همی زان نبردش سرآمد قفیز: همی is an emphatic/continuative particle (‘thus/indeed’); زان = ‘from that / thereupon’; نبردش = نبرد (battle) with enclitic -ش ‘his/its’ = ‘that battle’; سرآمد here means ‘conclusion/end/result’ (not only ‘foremost’); قفیز is an obscure/rare noun or name. Together: ‘Thus from that battle the conclusion (or end) came for Qafiz/that named thing.’

sentences.s189-s37: Thus a very wise man told this tale: that the mountain received punishment because of its evil disposition.
sentences.s189-s33: When Rīvuniz drew near from afar, that bent Shiz raised (his weapon).
sentences.s189-s34: From above he smote (him) with a dagger upon him, so that with a Roman rag his head was stitched.
sentences.s189-s35: He fell and his swift steed turned back from him; Rīvū's head also came to the ground.
sentences.s189-s36: When Tūs looked up from where he was, that mountain became unseen to his eyes.
sentences.s189-s29: Rider, if I were to make the steed lifeless with an arrow, what would you say, you wretch who has seen (such) deeds?
sentences.s189-s30: He said to him, 'Attack the man!' — lest Tus's heart be burned because of him.
sentences.s189-s31: He knows that you were true at heart, that you wished to be reconciled with him.
sentences.s189-s32: Thus he brought such a war against you, and thereby brought disgrace upon your brother.
sentences.s189-s17: Tūs's groom came again; he kept troubling the heavens with idle whispers.
sentences.s189-s18: From the Charm road he went up toward Sepdkuh; his heart was full of cruelty and he became distraught.
sentences.s189-s19: When he saw him fall from the high blade, he drew up the Kian bow.
sentences.s189-s20: Thus spoke Takhvār, battle‑seasoned, for Tūs had been humbled by those words.
sentences.s189-s21: That a horseman has come, and Bahram is not (here); I have a stout heart, and Pedram is not present.
sentences.s189-s22: Look, so that perhaps your memory may return and you remember who he is; he is clad head to toe in iron — for what purpose is that?
sentences.s189-s23: Thus the wretch replied to him: 'This one is of the Reyuni-kind — stout and a horseman.'
sentences.s189-s24: He has forty sisters like the joyful spring; besides this one there is no son in his lineage.
sentences.s189-s5: Bahrām said to him, “O champion, do nothing that will darken your soul.”
sentences.s189-s6: Fear God, the sun and the moon; bring your heart to pity at the sight of the king.
sentences.s189-s7: For he is his bond and his twin; he is a famed horseman and a warrior.
sentences.s189-s8: If a single rider from the midst of the army goes to that skilled son of the king…
sentences.s189-s9: No one escapes his grip alive; sorrow will indeed come upon the joyful heart.
sentences.s189-s10: The spahbed became disturbed by his words; there was no counsel — Bahram, the hero, had no match for him.
sentences.s189-s11: He commanded that several of the named (men) ride forth toward the high mountain.
sentences.s189-s12: From the numerous troops they rushed forth; they raised battle and turned it against (him).
sentences.s189-s13: Bahram Gard said to them thus: "Do not show any leniency in this matter, O wise ones."
sentences.s189-s14: Know (it): that mountain is Kay Khosrow's very head; one hair of him is worth more than a hundred flanks.
sentences.s189-s15: Whoever saw Siavash's face did not find rest after that sight.
sentences.s189-s16: When Bahram gave this sign of descending, the rebels (the proud-necked) turned back from the road.
sentences.s189-s25: He is deceptive, Rīman, and a flatterer; brave and young, and the son‑in‑law of Tūs.
sentences.s189-s26: Farud said thus to the discerning man: that this must not be heard at the time of battle.
sentences.s189-s27: When he comes into battle, I will put him to sleep on the laps of the sisters.
sentences.s189-s28: If an ill‑doer should act so, may my stratagem be set aside; if he remains alive, do not count me among the dead.
sentences.s189-s1: Then he said thus to the rebels: "O renowned, proud-necked ones!"
sentences.s189-s2: I will make one man famous and longing for a name, who from here will set his face toward that Turk.
sentences.s189-s3: He shall cut off his head with a dagger from his body and bring it before me to this assembly.
sentences.s189-s4: He closed the middle among them there with Riyuniz; thus from that battle Qafiz reached its end.
